items.title: TWST re-rated from 3.7x to 11.6x EV/Sales since initiation — author now trimming
items.idea: Since going long Twist Bioscience last winter at ~3.7-3.8x NTM EV/Sales, the multiple has expanded to 11.6x while shares are up over 3x (author +260% on cost basis, already trimmed once at +312% on June 26). Weekly RSI recently hit the 80s with notable distribution last week, and the stock sits 89% above its 200-day SMA. The author now frames this as time to protect cost basis rather than add.
items.angle: A clean case study in multiple-expansion vs. fundamentals: revenue is still growing mid-teens with no profitability, so the entire re-rating is sentiment/flow-driven — which means it round-trips as easily as it ran.
items.tweetA: TWST's re-rating: 3.7x EV/Sales to 11.6x NTM in under a year, on a company still burning cash growing revenue at a mid-teens clip. That's not a fundamentals re-rating, that's a multiple-expansion trade — and multiple-expansion trades mean-revert. $TWST
items.tweetB: Author up 260% on cost basis, already trimmed once at +312%, and is now talking about protecting the basis instead of adding. That sequencing — bank some, then talk exit — is usually the smart-money tell you see mid-cycle, not late-cycle. $TWST

charts.desc: Annotated daily chart (author's transaction history) + weekly RSI chart showing overbought reading in the 80s. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Iran isn't closing Hormuz, it's trying to own the permission slip
items.idea: Iran's IRGC declared the Strait of Hormuz 'closed' after striking a vessel for using an 'unapproved route' — converting an open international waterway into a discretionary permissions regime rather than a physical blockade. The U.S. hit roughly 140 targets overnight in response, and Iran claimed retaliatory strikes on five Gulf states (Bahrain, Kuwait, Jordan, Qatar, Oman), pulling host-nation infrastructure into the pressure campaign.
items.angle: This reframes Hormuz risk from a binary 'closed or open' question to a control-point contest — which changes how you'd hedge it (war-risk insurance and AIS tanker tracking, not a closure headline).
items.tweetA: Iran isn't trying to close Hormuz, it's trying to own the permission slip. 'Unapproved route' as the trigger for a strike converts an open strait into a discretionary chokepoint — and that's scarier for oil than an actual blockade, because it never has to be tested to work.
items.tweetB: 140 targets hit, 5 Gulf states hit back in claimed retaliation, and war-risk insurance still hasn't caught up to a multi-state escalation. If Bahrain or Qatar basing gets disrupted, the marginal barrel from the whole Gulf — not just Iran — becomes the story. $BZ $CL

charts.desc: No embedded chart images — text/data brief with linked sources (Axios, FT, EIA, Bloomberg).

items.title: Tanker transits through Hormuz: 35 → 14 → 2 in under a week
items.idea: AIS-tracked tanker transits through Hormuz collapsed from 35 vessels to 14 in a single day last week, and only 2 product tankers were seen approaching early Sunday. Hormuz carries roughly 20% of global petroleum liquids consumption. The next concrete test is the Monday, July 13 transit window — whether ships anchored off Oman, Khor Fakkan and Fujairah resume AIS-visible movement.
items.angle: The tradeable signal is the AIS data, not the rhetoric from either government — today's transit count (or lack of one) tells you whether 'freedom of navigation' is real or just a talking point.
items.tweetA: The actual catalyst isn't a headline, it's a data point: do AIS-tracked tankers anchored off Oman/Fujairah start moving today, July 13? No movement and the U.S. 'strait is open' claim is dead in the water — literally. Watch the transit count before the close, not CENTCOM's press release.
items.tweetB: 35 tankers a day to 14 to 2 — that's the real Hormuz chart, not the price of Brent. 20% of global petroleum liquids runs through this waterway, and insurers already repriced before a single official declared anything. $CL $USO

charts.desc: No embedded charts, but the underlying tanker-transit data (via SCMP/Guardian) is chart-worthy if you want to plot 35→14→2.

items.title: TSMC and ASML this week: MS models a beat on both, with capex commentary the real swing factor
items.idea: Morgan Stanley models ASML Q2 sales at €9bn, 3% ahead of consensus, with EPS 5% better than estimates on strong EUV/DUV shipments. TSMC is expected to post 68-69% gross margins (above street), with raised guidance, higher capex spending, and commentary on tight CoWoS capacity — though a EUV supply shortage could cap capex upside. The SOX sits at a neutral 49.78 RSI heading into both prints.
items.angle: TSMC and ASML function as the two highest-signal gatekeepers on whether the AI capex cycle is still accelerating or just catching up to consensus — a neutral SOX RSI into binary prints is a setup, not a forecast.
items.tweetA: ASML: MS models €9bn Q2 sales, 3% above consensus, EPS beat of 5%. TSMC: 68-69% gross margin, above street, with raised capex guidance. These two prints this week decide whether the AI capex story is still compounding or just consensus catching up. $ASML $TSM
items.tweetB: SOX sits at 49.78 RSI — dead neutral — right before TSMC and ASML report. That's the market genuinely undecided on chips into two prints that set the capex tone for 2H26. Neutral RSI plus a binary earnings setup is exactly when vol sellers get run over.

charts.desc: Text-only preview; no embedded charts in this section.

items.title: Author counts Hormuz as 'closed' and 'reopened' ~50 times — markets may be habituating to the headline
items.idea: The author (with acknowledged exaggeration) frames the repeated Hormuz open/close announcements as theater by both governments for domestic audiences rather than substantive negotiation, arguing time is 'running out' for an actual deal while both sides keep attacking during the nominal ceasefire.
items.angle: A useful contrarian gut-check against the more urgent Pressure Point framing: if traders start pricing repeated closure headlines as noise, the real risk is the one time the announcement isn't theater.
items.tweetA: Strait of Hormuz has been 'closed' and 'reopened' often enough this month that traders are starting to treat CENTCOM statements like whisper numbers — priced in before they print. The risk isn't the next headline, it's the one nobody reacts to anymore.
items.tweetB: Either this ceasefire theater ends in an actual deal, or the market's habituation to 'Hormuz closed again' headlines is exactly the setup for the one time it's not theater. Complacency compounds until the day it doesn't. $USO

items.title: Yen at 52-week low + VIX at multi-month low + tight credit = the Aug-2024 carry-unwind setup, rebuilt
items.idea: FXY closed at 56.95 (52-week low: 56.40), the VIX collapsed 27% from its June 10 peak of 22.22 to 16.15, and the HYG/LQD ratio sits near recent highs — signaling credit is barely compensating for volatility risk. The author argues this three-way alignment (weak funding currency, low equity vol, tight credit) historically precedes reverse-carry episodes like August 2024's yen unwind.
items.angle: Independent of the JOJO ETF pitch wrapped around it, the three-asset alignment is a real, checkable pattern — worth tracking as a leading indicator regardless of what vehicle you'd use to express it.
items.tweetA: Yen at 56.95 (52-week low: 56.40), VIX down 27% from its June peak to 16.15, credit spreads near their tightest. That's the exact three-way alignment that preceded the August 2024 carry unwind. The catalyst is unknown. The setup is not.
items.tweetB: What happens when the funding currency, equity vol, and credit spreads all agree the current regime is durable — at the same time? Historically: nothing, until credit reprices to vol and vol reprices to the yen, fast, in that order. Nobody's asset class is pricing that sequence right now.

charts.desc: Two-panel chart: VIX collapse to 16.15 alongside FXY at 56.95 near its 52-week low — plus separate FXY 52-week chart and HYG/LQD ratio chart. 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING (the two-panel vol/yen setup).

items.title: Kalshi odds on Hormuz normalizing by Sept 1 collapsed from 69% to 13% in under three weeks
items.idea: A Kalshi contract on Strait of Hormuz traffic normalizing by September 1 rose as high as 69% on June 25, but has fallen to 13% today — the lowest print since the contract launched, and less than half of last week's level.
items.angle: A cleaner, higher-frequency read on Hormuz risk than any newsletter take or oil price candle — prediction markets are moving faster and harder than realized vol in crude.
items.tweetA: Kalshi odds on Hormuz traffic normalizing by Sept 1: 69% on June 25, 13% today. A 56-point collapse in under three weeks, and the lowest print since the contract launched. Prediction markets are pricing this crisis more honestly than oil vol is. $CL
items.tweetB: Nobody's talking about the Kalshi number. Everyone's watching Brent. But a 69%-to-13% swing in a 'will shipping normalize' contract is a cleaner tell on this crisis's staying power than any single price candle — and it's moving in one direction only.

charts.desc: References BofA/DB one-pagers and cheat sheets (not reproducible from preview) — charts inside, worth opening.

items.title: China Q2 preview: exports +17% YoY vs. retail sales +0.2% YoY — a 17-point consumer gap
items.idea: Deutsche Bank forecasts China real GDP growth slowing to 4.4% in Q2 from 5% in Q1, with June industrial production improving to 4.8% YoY and exports up 17% YoY — but retail sales are expected to rebound to just 0.2% YoY, and investment remains soft at -4.0% YTD YoY.
items.angle: The 17-point gap between export growth and retail sales growth says China's 'recovery' is still almost entirely externally driven — the domestic consumer trade isn't participating, which matters for anyone positioned for a China reopening/consumption story.
items.tweetA: China Q2 preview: exports +17% YoY, retail sales +0.2% YoY. That's a 17-point gap between what China sells abroad and what its own households buy. This isn't a broad recovery — it's an export machine running on cost advantage the consumer isn't sharing in.
items.tweetB: DB sees China GDP slowing to 4.4% in Q2 from 5% in Q1, with investment still down -4.0% YTD YoY even as industrial production accelerates to 4.8%. Running existing capacity harder while investment shrinks is a strange combination for a 'recovery.'

charts.desc: Text forecast table referenced (DB one-pager) — not reproduced in preview.

items.title: ETH retesting June's support-turned-resistance zone with the 55-day EMA sitting right on top
items.idea: Ethereum is retesting the former support zone that flipped into resistance in June, and the triple EMA band (21/55/200-day) shows the 55-day EMA sitting directly at that same level — two independent signals (horizontal structure + moving average) aligning to increase the odds of a rejection rather than a breakout.
items.angle: A concrete level to watch this week for anyone trading ETH: confluence between a structural resistance zone and a moving-average resistance raises the bar for a clean breakout on the first attempt.
items.tweetA: ETH is retesting the same zone that flipped from support to resistance in June — and the 55-day EMA now sits right on top of it. Two independent signals agreeing on one level is exactly the kind of confluence that raises rejection odds. $ETH
items.tweetB: Either ETH breaks the June resistance zone with the 55-day EMA sitting right there, or this becomes another failed retest. Confluence like this rarely produces a clean breakout on the first try. $ETH

charts.desc: Triple EMA band chart (21/55/200-day) showing the retest at the resistance zone. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Beijing's new white paper on 'fairer' global governance, read the same month the US is bombing Iran to keep a strait open
items.idea: China's State Council Information Office published a white paper last month — 'More Just and Equitable Global Governance: China's Principles, Proposals and Actions.' Frankopan argues most such documents disappear into the gloom, but this one is worth reading not for its policy content but as a window into how Beijing's leadership is thinking about the future global order.
items.angle: Worth reading in the same week as the Hormuz crisis — Beijing is positioning itself rhetorically as the fair-arbiter alternative narrative exactly as Washington enforces Gulf shipping rules with airstrikes.
items.tweetA: Beijing drops a white paper titled 'More Just and Equitable Global Governance' the same month the US is bombing Iran to keep a shipping lane open. Not a coincidence in messaging — China is positioning itself as the rules-based alternative while Washington enforces the current rules with airstrikes.
items.tweetB: Most China white papers are noise. This one's worth reading for a different reason: not what it promises, but what it reveals about how Beijing's leadership actually thinks the post-US order should be built. Read the mechanism, not the marketing.
